mnemonics.title: Message Queue Operations
mnemonics.content: SEND waits, RECEIVE reads - Both can BLOCK when queue state impedes. For ENQUEUE think ENTRY, for DEQUEUE think EXIT from the back.

mnemonics.title: System V vs POSIX Queues
mnemonics.content: System V: 'msgget msgsnd msgrcv msgctl' (get-send-receive-control). POSIX: 'mq_open mq_send mq_receive mq_close' (open-send-receive-close). POSIX is simpler with file-based approach.

mnemonics.title: Circular Queue Front/Rear
mnemonics.content: Enqueue moves REAR forward (like reading direction). Dequeue moves FRONT forward. When either reaches SIZE-1, wraps to 0 using modulo.

acronyms.usage: Message queues are a form of IPC enabling process communication
Standard API for Unix-like systems including message queues

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a clock face. When front or rear reaches 12 (end), it wraps to 1 (beginning). Use (index + 1) % size formula to calculate next position.
Think of a restaurant kitchen: Producer (chef) puts food on counter (queue), Consumer (waiter) takes from counter. Semaphores track empty plates and full plates available.
Draw three boxes: Producer → Message Queue (middle buffer) → Consumer. Arrows show data flow. Queue acts as a 'post office' between sender and receiver.

analogies.analogy: Like a postal system - you drop a letter (message) in a mailbox (queue), it stays there until the recipient (consumer) picks it up. You don't wait at the mailbox.
Like leaving a voicemail - you speak your message and hang up (enqueue), then continue with your day. The recipient listens later (dequeue). Neither waits for the other.
Like emergency room patients - someone with critical injury (high priority) gets treated before someone with minor flu (low priority), regardless of arrival order.
Like a shipping hub/distribution center - receives packages from senders, sorts them, routes to correct destinations, handles any transformations needed.
